title: Live-in relationships - what legal protection actually exists
p1: Most people assume live-in relationships have either no legal standing or full marital rights. Both are wrong—and the gap between them matters more than most realise.
p2: A common belief is that live-in partners have no legal protection at all. That is only partly true. Indian courts, in specific situations, have extended certain safeguards under the Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act, 2005. This can include residence rights and financial support. But there is a condition—courts must see the relationship as “in the nature of marriage.” Factors like shared household, duration, social recognition, and financial interdependence are considered. Casual cohabitation does not qualify, which is where most assumptions fail.
p3: Practical takeaway: rights are not automatic; they are proven through facts of the relationship. If someone is in such an arrangement, clarity helps—shared lease agreements, joint financial records, and consistency in how the relationship is presented publicly can matter if disputes arise. Many people only discover this during conflict, when documentation is already missing. Legal outcomes often depend on perception + evidence, not just the label partners use.
conclusion: Which aspect of live-in relationships do you think most people misunderstand—the rights, the recognition, or the proof required in court cases?
